steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence says the 1991 chimpanzee study's claim of right-handedness should be treated skeptically "given that ______". The blank must supply the specific reason for skepticism. The passage already provides that reason: a meta-analysis established that 176 individuals is the minimum sample size needed to trust a population-level handedness finding, and the 1991 study had only 14 individuals.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — right-handedness does not occur frequently enough among chimpanzees to reliably appear in a sample of only 14 individuals.: This assumes right-handedness is rare in chimpanzees, which is exactly what the study was trying to determine — the passage never establishes this as a known fact. The argument would be circular. ✗
  • Choice B — the apparent difference between the two studies' results may be partly attributable to the 1988 study using a different standard to determine handedness than the 1991 study did.: The passage does not mention different standards for measuring handedness between the two studies. This introduces an unsupported comparison. ✗
  • Choice C — the study that did not find right-handedness in gorillas was also based on an insufficient population size.: The 1988 Kline study (gorillas, no right-handedness found) is not said to have an insufficient sample — only the 1991 study's sample is described. This also misdirects the skepticism away from the 1991 claim. ✗
  • Choice D — the sample size on which the claim is based is far below the threshold identified in the meta-analysis.: This directly connects the 1991 study's 14-individual sample to the 176-individual threshold from the meta-analysis, which is exactly the logical basis the passage supplies for skepticism. ✓
That is the correct answer.
